# AI Search Visibility Scorecard

A portfolio project: a working measurement framework for brand visibility in AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Google AI Overviews, Gemini, Perplexity, Copilot), built as the kind of self-serve weekly scorecard a growth marketing analytics team would run. It tracks five Adobe products against their competitive categories on simulated data.

**Everything in this app is simulated.** The dataset is generated by a seeded script ([scripts/generate-data.mjs](scripts/generate-data.mjs)), so it is reproducible and contains no real answer-engine sampling, no Adobe internal data, and no claims about real brand performance. The measurement framework itself is real: metric definitions, sampling design, and uncertainty handling follow published industry practice, cited on the Methodology page. This is an independent project, not affiliated with or endorsed by Adobe.

## Why this exists

AI answer engines synthesize responses instead of ranking links. That breaks the standard search-marketing toolkit: there are no impression counts, no rank tracking, and answers change from run to run. Measuring brand presence in this channel means inventing the metrics, sampling with stated uncertainty, and connecting visibility to business outcomes through explicit assumptions rather than click-path attribution.

A team taking this seriously in 2026 already has a first-generation version of this measurement. This app is positioned as the hardening pass on that kind of foundation: it adds uncertainty quantification, controlled experiments, and drift monitoring to the visibility metrics a v1 stack typically starts with.

This app shows what that framework looks like in practice, end to end:

- **Weekly scorecard** (Overview): a Visibility Score composite per product per engine, week-over-week movement, quarterly targets, and an insight feed written the way you would brief a marketing leader.
- **Competitive context** (Categories): share of voice, position, and sentiment against named competitors in each product category.
- **The measurement unit** (Prompt Panel): a fixed panel of 40 prompts re-asked many times per week per engine, with Wilson confidence intervals on every mention rate because single runs of a stochastic system are not data.
- **Business connection** (Outcomes): a proxy chain from visibility to sessions to signups to a gross-new-ARR-style figure, with every assumption exposed as a slider and a sensitivity table showing which assumption matters most.
- **Learning loops** (Experiments): treated-vs-control prompt panel designs with pooled pre/post reads, including an honestly reported null result.
- **The fine print** (Methodology): metric formulas, sampling design, vocabulary aligned to Adobe LLM Optimizer's published terms, ten known limitations, and full data provenance.

## Running it

```bash
npm install
npm run data   # regenerate the simulated dataset (deterministic, seeded)
npm run dev    # http://localhost:3000
```

Stack: Next.js 16 (App Router), React 19, Tailwind 4, Recharts. Data is static JSON generated at `src/data/`.

## Honest attribution

I directed the measurement methodology, the dataset design, and every editorial decision; Claude (Anthropic) implemented the code and drafted copy under my review. The research grounding (Adobe LLM Optimizer vocabulary, GEO measurement practice, published AI-traffic statistics) was gathered from public sources, which are cited on the Methodology page.
